---
title: "Special occasions"
format: html
---

# Special Occasions and Recovery

Special occasions and the celebrations that come with them can be challenging for people in recovery. Many factors can increase the risk of relapse during this time.

Think of a recent or upcoming holiday, celebration, or special event and then review the list below and check any items that may affect your recovery during this type of event.

![](https://github.com/jjcurtin/aud_support/blob/main/modules/images/birthdayhat.png?raw=true){fig-align="center"}


## Risk Checklist

- [ ] More alcohol and drugs at parties  
- [ ] Shortage of money due to travel or gift buying  
- [ ] Increased stress from a hectic pace (traffic, crowds, scheduling)  
- [ ] Normal routines disrupted  
- [ ] Stopping exercise  
- [ ] Not attending recovery meetings (e.g., AA)  
- [ ] Missing therapy or counseling sessions  
- [ ] Party-focused environments  
- [ ] Increased contact with family  
- [ ] Strong emotions triggered by holiday memories  
- [ ] Increased anxiety about triggers or cravings  
- [ ] Frustration from lack of time to meet responsibilities  
- [ ] Coping with high-risk celebrations (e.g., New Year’s Eve)  
- [ ] Extra free time without structure  
- [ ] **Other:** ___________________________________________

---

## Total Your Score

Count the number of items you checked:

**Total: ______**

---

## Relapse Risk Assessment

### Mild Risk (1–3 items)
This occasion may create a **slight increase in relapse risk**.

- Stay aware and maintain your current recovery routine.

---

### Moderate Risk (4–6 items)
This occasion is adding **significant stress** to your life.

- Your relapse risk depends on how well you cope with that stress. 

- **Action needed:** Plan ahead and strengthen your recovery supports.

---

### Severe Risk (7 or more items)
This occasion is creating a **high-risk period** for relapse.

- This level of stress requires **extra attention and care**.  

- **Action needed:** Increase structure, support, and monitoring immediately.


::: callout

## Protecting Your Recovery

During high-risk times:

- Add extra recovery meetings  
- Schedule your days to reduce unstructured time  
- Stay connected to your counselor or therapist  
- Use the coping tools that have helped you remain abstinent  

:::
